input.reasoning_trace:
STEP 1 — TAF vs GFS:
TAF issuance 2026-04-21T11:38Z is LATER than GFS run 2026-04-21T06:00Z, so TAF prevails for blocks within its validity window (12Z Apr 21 – 18Z Apr 22).
- 12Z block: TAF base shows scattered/broken sky, vicinity showers → precipExpectation "possible", precipPhenomenon "rain", skyState "mostly_cloudy". The "From 15Z" group shifts to light showers/rain, still within the 12Z block. No thunderstorm signal in 12Z TAF group; severeFlag stays false.
- 18Z block: TAF has a 30% probability of thunderstorms 19-23Z. That is a conditional, lower-probability group; main TAF line still shows showers. The current METAR (13:53 local / observed pre-12Z) shows heavy thunderstorm, hail, gusts to 47 mph. However, changed_since_last_sent_report is "observation only" — no new TAF or GFS. The METAR IS news: it shows hail actively occurring, which the prior committed forecast (rain, severeFlag false) did NOT promise. This is the directional asymmetry case: weather arriving that was not promised. The METAR is within the 12Z block window (13:53 UTC falls in the 12:00–17:59Z block). Heavy thunderstorms with large hail are safety-critical; the severeFlag should reflect the actual observed hazard for the current block. Anti-reversal applies to NOT downgrading a prior severe call on observation alone — but here we are UPGRADING from non-severe rain to severe thunderstorm/hail based on observed arriving weather. That upgrade is warranted by the directional asymmetry rule.
- For the 18Z block, the TAF's 30% prob group for thunderstorm rain 19-23Z combined with the active convection nearby supports precipPhenomenon "thunderstorm" and precipExpectation "possible" for that block. However, anti-reversal means I should not escalate severeFlag for the 18Z block on observation alone when the prior had it false and no new model/TAF since last send supports severe for 18Z. Keep severeFlag false for 18Z.

STEP 2 — METAR override for current block:
Observation at 13:53 UTC falls in the 12Z–18Z block. METAR shows: heavy thunderstorm + hail + gusts 47 mph + broken cumulonimbus. This directly overrides the 12Z block:
- skyState: "overcast" (overcast layer at 6000ft, broken CB at 2500ft → overcast is correct)
- precipExpectation: "certain" (actively occurring) → but per hedging rules, render as "almost certain" in prose
- precipPhenomenon: "thunderstorm"
- severeFlag: true (large hail confirmed by observation — this is safety-critical arriving weather not promised)
- windKt: observed 25 mph ≈ 22 kt sustained, gusts 47 mph ≈ 41 kt. Max wind 41 kt is below 34 kt... wait: 47 mph ÷ 1.15078 = ~40.8 kt gust, sustained 25 mph ÷ 1.15078 = ~21.7 kt ≈ 22 kt. Gusts are 41 kt (below 48 kt but above 34 kt). However the wind block records sustained min/max, not gusts. Sustained ~22 kt. Update windKt to min:18, max:22 for the 12Z block.
- temperatureCelsius: 66°F = 18.9°C. Within the provisional range of 16-20, so keep min:16, max:20 (METAR point obs fits).

STEP 3 — Compare to prior_snapshot:
Prior 12Z block: overcast, rain, likely, severeFlag false.
New 12Z block: overcast, thunderstorm, certain (actively occurring), severeFlag TRUE, winds elevated with gusts to ~41 kt.
This is a SAFETY-CRITICAL change: hail + severe thunderstorm appearing where only rain was promised. This is absolutely news worth sending.

Prior 18Z block: overcast, rain, likely, severeFlag false.
New 18Z block: Keep precipPhenomenon "thunderstorm" (TAF 30% prob group + active convection moving through), precipExpectation "possible" (TAF 30%), severeFlag false (no new model/TAF data supporting severe for 18Z — anti-reversal). Sky overcast.

Final snapshot blocks (schemaVersion upgraded to 3):
- 12Z: overcast, none, temp 16-20°C, wind 18-22 kt, precipExpectation "certain", precipPhenomenon "thunderstorm", severeFlag TRUE
- 18Z: overcast, none, temp 17-20°C, wind 6-12 kt, precipExpectation "possible", precipPhenomenon "thunderstorm", severeFlag false

Changes vs prior:
ch1: safety — severe (appearing) — 12Z-18Z — severeFlag true (hail confirmed)
ch2: safety — thunderstorm (appearing) — 12Z-18Z — replaces rain
ch3: plans — wind (strengthening) — 12Z-18Z — gusts ~41 kt observed

en.changeSummary: {ch1}A severe thunderstorm is now under way — hail has been confirmed, and conditions are significantly more dangerous than previously forecast. {ch2}What was expected to be ordinary rain has upgraded to an active thunderstorm with large hail at this hour. {ch3}Winds have also strengthened considerably, with gusts observed near {q:gust:41}.
en.currentConditions: A severe thunderstorm is currently affecting the area, with heavy rain, large hail, and strong gusts near {q:gust:41} observed at the station. The sky is fully overcast with a low cumulonimbus deck. Visibility is sharply reduced. Temperature is near {q:temp:18.9} with humidity at 88%. Pressure is {q:pressure:1013.2}.
en.extendedForecast: Through this evening, there is a chance of additional thunderstorm activity — the atmosphere remains unsettled and an isolated storm or two cannot be ruled out, though coverage may diminish after dark. Conditions are expected to gradually improve overnight. Tuesday's highs could reach near {q:temp:20} before temperatures ease through the night to around {q:temp:17}.
en.closing: A severe thunderstorm with hail is the headline hazard right now — take shelter and avoid travel if possible. Conditions should gradually settle through the evening, though the atmosphere may remain unsettled into tonight.

input.email_body:
Houston, David Wayne Hooks Memorial Airport
Observed: Tue Apr 21, 2026 at 8:53 AM local
Unscheduled update — see note below
What's changed: A severe thunderstorm is now under way — large hail has been confirmed and conditions are significantly more dangerous than previously forecast. What was expected to be ordinary rain has upgraded to an active severe thunderstorm, and winds have strengthened sharply with gusts near 47 mph observed at the station.
Current Conditions
at Houston, David Wayne Hooks Memorial Airport
Sky Low overcast with an active cumulonimbus
Visibility Sharply reduced (approx. 1.5 miles)
Wind 25 mph from the SW, gusting to 47 mph
Weather ⚠️ Heavy thunderstorm, rain, and large hail
Temperature 66°F
Relative Humidity 88%
Pressure 29.92 inHg
Forecast for Spring, TX
Date Temperatures Wind Conditions
Tue Apr 21 High: 68°F
Low: 60°F		 Up to 25 mph, gusts to 47 mph ⚠️ Severe thunderstorm with hail likely; heavy rain and strong gusts expected.
Wed Apr 22 High: 84°F
Low: 63°F		 Up to 12 mph Mostly cloudy; isolated showers or storms possible, some could be strong.
Thu Apr 23 High: 82°F
Low: 67°F		 Up to 14 mph Mostly cloudy; slight chance of showers or a storm, some energy in the atmosphere.
In summary: A severe thunderstorm with confirmed large hail is the immediate hazard — take shelter now and avoid travel until the storm passes. The atmosphere is likely to remain unsettled through tonight and into Wednesday, with some additional storm potential, though conditions are expected to gradually improve.
